title: Jaundice and fatigue after travel

chart.oneLiner: 28M presents with a week of fatigue, nausea, right upper quadrant discomfort, dark urine, and now jaundice, following recent travel.

opening: My eyes have gone yellow, my urine is dark, and I've felt wiped out and queasy for a week.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 28-year-old man, fatigued, mildly jaundiced. Good historian.

HPI: A week of malaise, anorexia, nausea, low-grade fevers, and right upper quadrant discomfort; then dark urine, pale stools, and yellowing of the eyes/skin. On asking: recent travel to a region with poor sanitation and some questionable food/water (Hepatitis A risk); no IV drug use, but had a new tattoo abroad and unprotected sex (Hep B/C risks) — only if asked about exposures. Denies large acetaminophen use (but always verify). NO confusion/altered behavior (would suggest acute liver failure).
PMH: none. MEDS: occasional acetaminophen for headaches (confirm amount). SOCIAL: social alcohol, recent travel.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: jaundiced (scleral icterus), tired.
- Vitals: BP 120/76, HR 84, RR 14, 37.6°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Abdomen: tender hepatomegaly in the RUQ; no ascites; no stigmata of chronic liver disease.
- Neuro: alert, NO asterixis/encephalopathy.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the travel, tattoo, sexual exposures, or acetaminophen amount unless asked about travel/exposures or medications.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.LFTs: AST 1850, ALT 2200 (ALT>AST, markedly elevated — hepatocellular pattern), Alk Phos mildly elevated.
labs.Bilirubin: Total bilirubin elevated with a direct (conjugated) predominance.
labs.INR: Near normal (an elevated INR with encephalopathy would signal acute liver failure).
labs.Acetaminophen: Acetaminophen level NOT elevated (always checked to exclude APAP toxicity).
labs.ViralSerologies: Hepatitis A IgM positive (acute); Hepatitis B and C panels sent.
labs.US: RUQ ultrasound: hepatomegaly, patent vessels, no biliary obstruction.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Failure of R-wave amplitude to increase across V1-V4 (poor R-wave progression).
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Failure of R-wave amplitude to increase across V1-V4 (poor R-wave progression). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
searchTerms: poor precordial R wave progression 12 lead

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Cardiac apex pointing to the right (dextrocardia); check for situs and correct lead/marker placement.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Cardiac apex pointing to the right (dextrocardia); check for situs and correct lead/marker placement. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for the markedly elevated transaminases (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acute viral hepatitis (leading,7). Drug-induced/acetaminophen injury (CANT-MISS — must exclude,5). Alcoholic hepatitis (core,3). Autoimmune hepatitis (core,2). Ischemic hepatitis (core,2). Biliary obstruction (core,2). Names viral hepatitis with the hepatocellular (ALT>AST, very high) pattern while excluding APAP (core,4).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute viral hepatitis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Drug-induced/acetaminophen injury. Also reasonable: Alcoholic hepatitis; Autoimmune hepatitis; Ischemic hepatitis; Biliary obstruction.

coreActions.item: Names viral hepatitis with the hepatocellular (ALT>AST, very high) pattern while excluding APAP

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: LFTs incl. AST/ALT pattern (CANT-MISS,3). Bilirubin (fractionated) (2). INR/coags — synthetic function (3). ACETAMINOPHEN level (CANT-MISS,3). Viral hepatitis serologies — A/B/C (3). RUQ ultrasound (1). PENALTY: not checking an acetaminophen level in acute hepatocellular injury (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; LFTs; Bilirubin; INR; Acetaminophen; Viral serologies; US.

penalties.item: not checking an acetaminophen level in acute hepatocellular injury

contraindicatedTests: not checking an acetaminophen level in acute hepatocellular injury

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = poor R-wave progression across V1-V4. CREDIT: systematic rate & rhythm read (7); recognizes poor R-wave progression (5); gives a differential (prior anterior MI, LVH, lead placement) (3). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the abnormality (-3).
keyFindings: gives a differential (prior anterior MI, LVH, lead placement)
criticalFindings: Poor R-wave progression across the precordium.
commonMistakes: ignoring the precordial R-wave pattern altogether
steps.idealAnswer: Poor R-wave progression: R waves fail to grow normally across V1-V4.

criticalActions: Poor R-wave progression across the precordium.
coreActions: gives a differential (prior anterior MI, LVH, lead placement)

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = dextrocardia (cardiac apex on the right). CREDIT: recognizes dextrocardia (6); verifies side markers / lead placement before calling it (4). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the abnormality (-3).
keyFindings: verifies side markers / lead placement before calling it
commonMistakes: assuming a left-right marker error without confirming
steps.idealAnswer: Dextrocardia: cardiac apex on the right — confirm side markers and consider situs inversus.

coreActions: verifies side markers / lead placement before calling it

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: ALT>AST in the thousands, a negative acetaminophen level, and a positive Hepatitis A IgM. What is your diagnosis and what must you monitor?
steps.rubric: Acute viral hepatitis (Hepatitis A) (6). Monitors for acute liver failure — rising INR and encephalopathy/asterixis would change management (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute viral hepatitis (Hepatitis A). Monitor for progression to acute liver failure: trend the INR and watch for encephalopathy/asterixis, which would change management and prompt hepatology/transplant referral.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH viral hepatitis: supportive care – hydration, antiemetics, avoid hepatotoxins (CANT-MISS,6); confirm type with serologies (CANT-MISS,4); monitor INR/mental status for ALF (5); hepatology referral / transplant evaluation if coagulopathy or HE (4); counsel on transmission + vaccinate contacts / public-health reporting (3). PENALTY: continued hepatotoxins (alcohol/APAP) (-3); missing progression to acute liver failure (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute viral hepatitis (A/B) is usually supportive: hydration, antiemetics, rest, and avoid hepatotoxins (alcohol, acetaminophen dosing caution). Confirm the type with serologies (anti-HAV IgM; HBsAg/anti-HBc IgM). Monitor INR and mental status for acute liver failure – escalate to hepatology/transplant if coagulopathy or encephalopathy. Counsel on transmission; vaccinate contacts; report to public health.

coreActions.item: Monitor INR and mental status for ALF
Counsel on transmission and vaccinate contacts

optionalActions: Monitor INR and mental status for ALF
Counsel on transmission and vaccinate contacts

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 28M presents with a week of fatigue, nausea, right upper quadrant discomfort, dark urine, and now jaundice, following recent travel. The diagnosis is Acute viral hepatitis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyHistoryClues: recent travel to a region with poor sanitation and questionable food/water
new tattoo abroad and unprotected sex (Hep B/C exposures)
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: tender hepatomegaly in the RUQ; no ascites; no stigmata of chronic liver disease.
Abdomen: tender hepatomegaly in the RUQ; no ascites; no stigmata of chronic liver disease.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Acute viral hepatitis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Acute viral hepatitis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.

keyImageInterpretationFindings: Poor R-wave progression across the precordium.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Poor R-wave progression across the precordium.
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
